questions.question:
Corners are cut from an equilateral triangle to produce a regular convex hexagon as shown in the figure. The ratio of the area of the regular convex hexagon to the area of the original equilateral triangle is

A. 2:3    B. 3:4    C. 4:5    D. 5:6
questions.to_find: ratio of area of the regular convex hexagon to area of the original equilateral triangle

understand.plain: An equilateral triangle has each of its three corners snipped off symmetrically. The cut at each corner removes a small equilateral triangle, and the remaining shape is a regular convex hexagon. To find the area ratio, we compare the hexagon area to the total original triangle area.

keywords.explain: A six-sided polygon where all sides and angles are equal, and no interior angle exceeds 180 degrees.
keywords.example: A honeycomb cell is a regular hexagon; it can be formed by cutting the three corners of an equilateral triangle equally.

keywords.explain: A triangle in which all three sides are equal in length and all angles equal 60 degrees.
keywords.example: A triangle with side 3a has area (sqrt(3)/4)*(3a)^2 = (9*sqrt(3)/4)*a^2.

keywords.explain: Each corner removed is itself a small equilateral triangle. For a regular hexagon to result, all three corners must be identical cuts.
keywords.example: If the original triangle has side 3a, each corner cut has side a, giving three small triangles each of area (sqrt(3)/4)*a^2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 280'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='280' class='bg'/><polygon points='160,30 10,250 310,250' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,4'/><polygon points='160,30 110,114 210,114' fill='#D85A30' opacity='0.35' stroke='none'/><polygon points='10,250 76,250 43,192' fill='#D85A30' opacity='0.35' stroke='none'/><polygon points='310,250 244,250 277,192' fill='#D85A30' opacity='0.35' stroke='none'/><polygon points='110,114 210,114 277,192 244,250 76,250 43,192' class='hi'/><text x='160' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Hexagon</text><text x='160' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>cut corner</text><text x='37' y='245' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>cut</text><text x='283' y='245' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>cut</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Equilateral triangle with dashed outline; three red corner triangles removed to reveal the central regular hexagon shaded purple.

given.aim: find ratio of hexagon area to original equilateral triangle area

terms.meaning: Let the side of the original equilateral triangle be 3a, so we can cut equal pieces of side a from each corner.
terms.example: Side = 3a means each third along a side equals a.
terms.connects: sets up the size parameter for computing areas

terms.meaning: Each corner removed is an equilateral triangle of side a (one-third of the original side).
terms.example: Three such triangles are removed, one from each corner.
terms.connects: determines the area subtracted from the original triangle

terms.meaning: Area = (sqrt(3)/4)*s^2, where s is the side length.
terms.connects: core formula used to compute all required areas

given.plan: Express the hexagon area as (original triangle area) minus (3 corner triangle areas). Divide to get the ratio.

steps.title: Set up side lengths
steps.apply: Let original equilateral triangle have side 3a. Each corner cut removes an equilateral triangle of side a (trisecting each side gives the regular hexagon property).
steps.note: Trisecting is the unique cut that yields a regular hexagon.

steps.title: Compute original triangle area
steps.note: This is the full area before any cuts.

steps.title: Compute area of one corner triangle
steps.note: Each of the three corners is identical by symmetry.

steps.title: Compute hexagon area and ratio
steps.note: Ratio 2:3 means the hexagon covers two-thirds of the original triangle.

solution.result: Ratio of hexagon area to original triangle area = 2:3.

questions.formula_note: The area formula for an equilateral triangle of side s is (sqrt(3)/4)*s^2; trisecting sides ensures the three removed corners are identical equilateral triangles.

questions.question:
In the figure, O is the center of the circle and M and N lie on the circle. The area of the right triangle MON is 50 cm^2. What is the area of the circle in cm^2?

A. 2pi    B. 50pi    C. 75pi    D. 100pi
questions.to_find: area of the circle given that the area of right triangle MON is 50 cm^2

understand.plain: O is the center of a circle, and M and N are two points on the circle. Triangle MON has a right angle at O (the two legs OM and ON are radii, and the right angle is between them). Since OM = ON = r (the radius), the area of the right triangle is half the product of the two legs.

keywords.term: right triangle with legs as radii
keywords.explain: When the right angle is at the center O, the two sides OM and ON are both radii of length r, making the triangle an isosceles right triangle.
keywords.example: A right angle at O with OM = ON = r gives area = (1/2)*r*r = r^2/2.

keywords.explain: Every point on a circle is exactly one radius distance from the center; M and N on the circle means OM = ON = r.
keywords.example: If r = 10, then OM = ON = 10 cm.

keywords.explain: Area = pi*r^2, where r is the radius.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='300' class='bg'/><circle cx='150' cy='160' r='110' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='150,160 150,50 260,160' class='hi'/><line x1='150' y1='160' x2='150' y2='50' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='150' y1='160' x2='260' y2='160' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='150' y1='50' x2='260' y2='160' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='150' y='150' width='12' height='12' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='150' cy='160' r='5' class='ok'/><circle cx='150' cy='50' r='5' class='no'/><circle cx='260' cy='160' r='5' class='no'/><text x='140' y='182' class='lbl ink'>O</text><text x='140' y='42' class='lbl ink'>M</text><text x='267' y='165' class='lbl ink'>N</text><text x='60' y='130' class='sub mut'>r = OM</text><text x='170' y='220' class='sub mut'>r = ON</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Circle with center O; points M (top) and N (right) lie on the circle; right angle marked at O; triangle MON shaded purple with two radii as legs.

given.aim: find the area of the circle

terms.meaning: O is equidistant from all points on the circle; OM = ON = radius r.
terms.connects: establishes that the two legs of the right triangle are equal to the radius

terms.meaning: The right angle in triangle MON is at vertex O, so OM and ON are the two perpendicular legs.
terms.example: Legs OM and ON are perpendicular, so area = (1/2)*OM*ON = (1/2)*r*r = r^2/2.
terms.connects: links triangle area to r^2, allowing r^2 to be found

terms.meaning: The given area provides the equation r^2/2 = 50, so r^2 = 100.
terms.example: r^2/2 = 50 implies r^2 = 100, so r = 10 cm.
terms.connects: directly gives the value needed to compute the circle's area

given.plan: Use the right-triangle area formula with both legs equal to r to express area in terms of r^2. Solve for r^2, then compute pi*r^2.

steps.title: Identify the two legs of the right triangle
steps.formula_raw: OM = ON = r (radii of the circle)
steps.apply: Since O is the center and M, N lie on the circle, OM = ON = r. The right angle is at O, so the two legs are OM and ON.
steps.note: The hypotenuse MN is a chord of the circle, not a radius.

steps.title: Apply triangle area formula to get r^2
steps.note: Both legs equal r because they are radii.

steps.title: Compute area of the circle
steps.note: The answer is 100pi regardless of whether r = 10 or -10 (only the positive root is physical).

questions.formula_note: The right triangle with both legs equal to the radius gives area = r^2/2; setting this equal to 50 directly yields r^2 = 100, and pi*r^2 = 100*pi.

questions.question:
The ratio of the area of the inscribed circle to the area of the circumscribed circle of an equilateral triangle is

A. 1/8    B. 1/6    C. 1/4    D. 1/2
questions.to_find: ratio of area of inscribed circle (incircle) to area of circumscribed circle (circumcircle) of an equilateral triangle

understand.plain: Every triangle has two special circles: the incircle (the largest circle that fits inside, touching all three sides) and the circumcircle (the smallest circle that passes through all three vertices). For an equilateral triangle the two circles share the same center, and the ratio of their radii is exactly 1:2, so the area ratio is 1:4.

keywords.explain: The circle inside the triangle that is tangent to all three sides; its radius is called the inradius r.
keywords.example: For an equilateral triangle with side a, the inradius r = a / (2*sqrt(3)) = a*sqrt(3)/6.

keywords.explain: The circle that passes through all three vertices of the triangle; its radius is called the circumradius R.
keywords.example: For an equilateral triangle with side a, the circumradius R = a / sqrt(3) = a*sqrt(3)/3.

keywords.explain: If two circles have radii r and R, the ratio of their areas is (pi*r^2) / (pi*R^2) = (r/R)^2.
keywords.example: If r/R = 1/2 then the area ratio is 1/4.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 290'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='290' class='bg'/><circle cx='150' cy='155' r='110' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='150,45 260,235 40,235' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='150' cy='155' r='55' fill='#CECBF6' fill-opacity='0.5' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='150' cy='155' r='4' class='ok'/><text x='152' y='152' class='sub ink'>center</text><line x1='150' y1='155' x2='205' y2='155' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='170' y='148' class='sub no'>r</text><line x1='150' y1='155' x2='150' y2='45' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><text x='153' y='100' class='sub ok'>R</text><text x='30' y='270' class='sub mut'>Incircle (inner, r)</text><text x='170' y='270' class='sub mut'>Circumcircle (outer, R)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Equilateral triangle with the small incircle (purple fill) tangent to all three sides and the large circumcircle passing through all three vertices, sharing the same center.

given.aim: find the ratio of areas of the incircle and circumcircle of an equilateral triangle

terms.meaning: For an equilateral triangle with side a, the inradius r = a / (2*sqrt(3)) = a*sqrt(3)/6.
terms.connects: gives r in terms of side a

terms.meaning: For an equilateral triangle with side a, the circumradius R = a / sqrt(3) = a*sqrt(3)/3.
terms.example: If a = 6, then R = 6*sqrt(3)/3 = 2*sqrt(3), confirming R = 2r.
terms.connects: gives R in terms of side a; note R = 2r always

terms.example: The circumradius is always twice the inradius for an equilateral triangle.
terms.connects: key result used to compute the area ratio as (r/R)^2 = 1/4

given.plan: Derive both radii for an equilateral triangle of side a using standard formulas, compute r/R, then square it to get the area ratio.

steps.title: Write inradius of equilateral triangle
steps.apply: For equilateral triangle with side a: inradius r = a*sqrt(3)/6.
steps.note: r equals one-third of the altitude h = a*sqrt(3)/2; specifically r = h/3.

steps.title: Write circumradius of equilateral triangle
steps.apply: For equilateral triangle with side a: circumradius R = a*sqrt(3)/3.
steps.note: R equals two-thirds of the altitude; R = 2h/3, so R = 2r.

steps.title: Compute r/R
steps.apply: r/R = (1/6) / (1/3) = 1/2. The inradius is half the circumradius.
steps.note: This 1:2 ratio is specific to equilateral triangles; general triangles have different ratios.

steps.title: Compute area ratio
steps.note: The incircle has exactly one-quarter the area of the circumcircle for any equilateral triangle.

solution.result: Ratio of incircle area to circumcircle area = 1/4.

questions.formula_note: For an equilateral triangle the inradius is half the circumradius (r = R/2), so the area ratio is (r/R)^2 = 1/4; new formula IDs equilateral-triangle-inradius and equilateral-triangle-circumradius are added.

formulas.name: Area of an Equilateral Triangle
formulas.plain: Area = (sqrt(3)/4) * s^2  where s is the side length

formulas.name: Area of a Right Triangle
formulas.plain: Area = (1/2) * leg1 * leg2  (the two legs are the sides that form the right angle)

formulas.name: Area of a Circle
formulas.plain: Area = pi * r^2  where r is the radius

formulas.name: Inradius of an Equilateral Triangle
formulas.plain: r = a*sqrt(3)/6 = a/(2*sqrt(3))  where a is the side length; equals one-third of the altitude

formulas.name: Circumradius of an Equilateral Triangle
formulas.plain: R = a*sqrt(3)/3 = a/sqrt(3)  where a is the side length; equals two-thirds of the altitude; R = 2r
